exemplar.title: Investigating the Relationship Between Voltage and Luminosity in an Incandescent Light Bulb

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Relationship Between Voltage and Luminosity in an Incandescent Light Bulb

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables are clearly labeled with units and uncertainties, facilitating precise recording.
Graph is well-labelled and includes an RÂ² value, enhancing clarity and precision.
Average calculations are shown with formulas, demonstrating correct numerical methodology.
weaknesses: Table formatting is inconsistent and values for some voltages (7.5 V, 8 V) are omitted.
Uncertainty propagation is not applied to averaged data or curve-fit parameters; no error bars.
No derivation or reporting of fit parameters (exponent, gradient) and their uncertainties.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication is clear and precise in parts (band 5â6) but significant omissions in uncertainty treatment and data processing quality place the overall performance at band 3â4. A score of 3 balances strong presentation against gaps in propagation and processing.

strengths: Clear and focused research aim correlating voltage and luminosity.
Comprehensive theoretical background including filament physics and energy considerations.
Procedure lists equipment, voltage steps and ambient-light control, aiding reproducibility.
weaknesses: Context remains broad without specifying filament geometry or precise voltage range.
Critical procedural details missing (sensor-to-bulb distance, voltage increment policy beyond 8 V).
Independent variable control is ambiguously described (battery vs. power supply).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall solidly in band 3â4: the research question is outlined in a broad context, the methodological description is reproducible with minor ambiguities, and considerations are described but not fully explained. A mid-band score of 3 reflects consistent performance with noted omissions.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly links the non-linear luminosity increase to the analysis and high RÂ² value.
Discussion connects findings to filament temperature and non-ohmic behavior relevant to accepted scientific context.
weaknesses: Does not report the fitted power-law exponent or its uncertainty to fully justify the claim.
Scientific context comparison is qualitative; lacks quantitative reference (e.g., StefanâBoltzmann law).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is consistent with the data and shows relevant scientific comparison (band 3â4), but missing quantitative parameters prevents it from reaching the top of the band. A score of 3 reflects a described but not fully justified conclusion.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (lux-meter accuracy, filament heating).
Proposes realistic improvements (use of calibrated photometer, cooling intervals).
weaknesses: Does not explain the quantitative impact of identified weaknesses on the results.
Suggested improvements lack explanation of how they would reduce error magnitudes.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Weaknesses and improvements are clearly stated but not explained in terms of their relative impact or error reduction (band 1â2). A score of 2 reflects realistic yet unelaborated evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: **Research question context** â The student clearly states an implicit research question (ârelationship between voltage and luminosity for an incandescent bulbâ) and gives some background about energy efficiency and filament physics. However, the discussion remains quite general (sustainability, engineering applications) rather than locating the question in a narrowly defined, quantitative context (e.g. specific filament geometry or precise electrical range). This matches the descriptor âoutlined within a broad context,â placing the work securely in band 3-4; a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

**Consideration of uncertainties** â Instrument and random uncertainties are identified for voltage and lux readings, and carried into the averaged table. However, no propagation, error bars or discussion of percentage error versus trend strength is attempted. These significant omissions lower the work to the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects that some but not comprehensive treatment is provided.

**Methodological considerations (relevance & sufficiency)** â The student notes control of ambient light, repeats three trials and explains why low-voltage data were excluded. They recognise the lux-meterâs limitations but do not quantify its accuracy, justify trial number, or discuss distance alignment error, so considerations are described rather than fully explained. This aligns with band 3-4; a middle score of 3 is warranted.

**Methodological weaknesses** â Specific issues are identified: lux-meter inaccuracy and filament heating between trials. These weaknesses are described clearly but their quantitative impact on results (e.g. percentage error) is not explained, satisfying the descriptor for band 3-4; a score of 3 reflects adequate but not analytical treatment.

**Methodological description for collecting data** â The procedure lists equipment, step-by-step actions, voltage increments and the use of a phone lux-meter. A competent peer could reproduce most stages, though key details such as the fixed distance between sensor and bulb, the exact voltage step size policy after 8 V, and camera calibration are missing. These few ambiguities mean reproducibility is likely but not guaranteed, fitting the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects the minor but notable omissions.

**Data processing quality** â Averages are correctly calculated and a power-curve fit with RÂ² is produced, indicating appropriate software use. Nonetheless, no calculation of gradient, power exponent, or uncertainty in fit is given; propagation is absent and a portion of processed data (7.5 V, 8 V) is mis-formatted. These gaps constitute âsome significant omissions,â placing the strand in band 3-4 with a score of 3.

**Suggested improvements** â The student suggests using a calibrated photometer and cooling intervals between trials, both realistic and relevant to the stated weaknesses. However, explanations of how these changes would quantitatively enhance precision are absent. Therefore the response fits the âdescribedâ level within band 3-4, earning a score of 3.

**Communication of data recording & processing** â Raw and processed tables include units and stated uncertainties, and the graph is neatly labelled with an RÂ² value. Although one table header is mis-aligned and a stray symbol appears, overall presentation is both clear (easy to follow) and precise (units, significant figures, distinguishes trials vs. averages). These minor lapses lower the ideal score slightly, so a band 5-6 placement with a score of 5 is justified.

**Scientific context comparison** â The student links findings to non-ohmic behaviour and references filament temperature effects, demonstrating some relevant comparison with accepted physics. The discussion is brief and qualitative; no quantitative reference (e.g. Stefan-Boltzmann law) is provided. Thus the work meets, but does not exceed, the âsome relevant comparisonâ level, meriting a score of 3 in band 3-4.

**Conclusion relevance & support** â The student restates that luminosity increases non-linearly with voltage and cites the high RÂ² value of the power fit, directly linking the claim to the analysis. While numerical parameters of the fit are absent, the conclusion remains consistent with the evidence. This fits the âdescribed but not fully justifiedâ descriptor of band 3-4; the stronger coherence earns a top-band score of 4.

annotations.comment: The introduction outlines the research aim but remains in general sustainability terms without a specific quantitative or contextual boundary for the filament or voltage range.
The title clearly specifies the investigationâs focus on voltageâluminosity, providing a precise and concise IA topic.
Controlling ambient light to 0 Lux is a solid methodological step to eliminate background interference.
The procedure lacks a specified distance between the sensor and bulb, which is critical for reproducibility of luminosity measurements.
Voltage increment protocol beyond 8 V is not defined, leaving ambiguity in data collection at higher voltages.
The photograph of the setup is clear and well-captioned, facilitating reader understanding of the apparatus.
The student articulates the aim to correlate voltage and luminosity in an incandescent bulb, guiding the reader clearly toward the investigationâs purpose.
The background includes relevant equations and filament physics, demonstrating sound theoretical grounding.
The theory section omits a derivation predicting the expected power-law exponent, limiting the link between theory and data fitting.
The description of the independent variable refers ambiguously to a battery rather than clearly stating control via the power supply.
The hypothesis logically connects Ohmâs Law and power to predict non-linear luminosity increase, showing integration of theory and expectation.
The raw data table is clearly labeled with units and uncertainties, aiding precise data recording.
Table header symbols (overlines, bold) are inconsistent; standardize formatting to avoid confusion.
Consider adding error bars or uncertainty columns directly in tables and on the graph to communicate variability.
The trial-1 data table is presented in isolation; combining all trials in a single table with trial labels would improve clarity.
The average table omits values for 7.5 V and 8 V, creating an incomplete dataset and potential bias.
The average calculation is clearly shown with a formula, demonstrating correct numerical methodology.
Uncertainty propagation is not applied when averaging; include a treatment of how Â±5 Lux affects the mean.
The graph is well-labeled with units and includes an RÂ² value, enhancing clarity and precision of data communication.
The text refers to the graph as âFigure 1â instead of âFigure 5,â causing confusion in cross-referencing.
Include a concise discussion of how the random and systematic uncertainties propagate into the curve fit parameters.
The evaluation identifies the luxâmeter appâs sensitivity issues and heating effects as specific systematic errors.
Suggested improvements (photometer, cooling intervals) are relevant but lack explanation of how they quantitatively reduce error.
The conclusion succinctly connects the non-linear trend to filament temperature and non-ohmic behavior, consistent with analysis.
The conclusion would be strengthened by reporting the fitted exponent and its uncertainty from the powerâlaw fit.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
